title_en: Arabia Before Islam — The Physical and Historical Setting
content_en:
Al-Rahiq al-Makhtum (The Sealed Nectar) by Safi al-Rahman al-Mubarakpuri, winner of the first-prize award at the World Muslim League's biography competition in 1979, begins with a thorough geographical and historical survey of the Arabian Peninsula as a necessary foundation for understanding the context in which Islam was revealed.

The Arabian Peninsula is bounded by the Red Sea to the west, the Gulf of Aden to the south, the Arabian Sea to the southeast, the Gulf of Oman and the Persian Gulf to the east, and the Fertile Crescent to the north. Covering approximately three million square kilometers, it is one of the largest peninsulas on earth. Geographically, it divides into several distinct regions: the Hejaz, a narrow coastal strip along the Red Sea where Mecca and Medina are located; the Najd plateau in the interior; Yemen in the fertile south; Hadramawt; and the eastern coastal region of al-Ahsa.

The climate of the Hejaz, particularly around Mecca, was harsh — extreme heat, scarce rainfall, and rocky terrain that resisted cultivation. This very harshness contributed to the unique cultural character of the Arab people who inhabited it. Without the agricultural wealth of Persia or the Nile Valley of Egypt, the Arabs of the Hejaz developed an economy centered on trade, pastoralism, and the annual pilgrimage to Mecca. The city of Mecca occupied a position of exceptional geographic importance as a crossroads of major trade routes connecting Yemen in the south with the Levant in the north, and facilitating commerce between East Africa, Persia, and the Mediterranean world.

Historically, Arabia had been home to a succession of significant civilizations. The Sabaean kingdom of Yemen — referenced in the Quran through the story of the Queen of Sheba (Bilqis) — was famous for its agricultural prowess and the Great Marib Dam. The Nabataean kingdom, centered at Petra in what is today Jordan, controlled northern trade routes before being absorbed into the Roman Empire. The Lakhimid and Ghassanid kingdoms formed buffer states between the great empires of Persia and Byzantium, often fighting as proxies in those empires' long struggle for dominance.

By the time of the Prophet's ﷺ birth in approximately 570 CE, the two superpowers flanking Arabia — the Sassanid Persian Empire to the northeast and the Byzantine Roman Empire to the northwest — were engaged in a prolonged, mutually exhausting conflict. This geopolitical reality would prove providential for the rise of Islam: within decades of the Prophet's ﷺ death, both empires would be too weakened to prevent the rapid expansion of the nascent Muslim state. Al-Mubarakpuri's attention to this historical background is not incidental — it demonstrates how the divine wisdom operates through historical circumstances, preparing the world for the reception of the final prophetic message at precisely the moment when no earthly power was strong enough to suppress it.
